items.title:
Handwritten skills are dead. Microsoft just taught them to self-evolve. 

Most agent skill docs are ...
items.content:
Handwritten skills are dead. Microsoft just taught them to self-evolve. 

Most agent skill docs are handwritten and brittle. They rarely improve once shipped.
 
Microsoft Research just released a paper fixing this. 

It treats the skill file as a frozen agent's trainable state. A second model reads execution traces. 

It then proposes small add, delete, or replace edits.
 
A candidate ships only if it beats the prior version. 

A held-out set decides every round.
 
The system borrows discipline from gradient descent.
 
> Edit budget acts as learning rate
> Rejected changes feed negative signal
> Slow updates capture long-term patterns
> Minibatches separate wins from failures
 
Called SkillOpt, it won or tied on 52 setups. 

Tests spanned six benchmarks and seven models.
 
On one frontier setup it added 23.5 points in chat. Coding harnesses gained up to 24.8.
 
The final artifact stays under 2,000 tokens. 

What ships when skills train themselves?

items.title:
Microsoft just turned AI agents from "please behave" into hard law. 

AI agents now execute real act...
items.content:
Microsoft just turned AI agents from "please behave" into hard law. 

AI agents now execute real actions inside companies. 

Just telling them to follow the rules fails 26.67% of the time.
 
Microsoft released the Agent Governance Toolkit. 

It enforces rules at the application layer, dropping violations to 0%.
 
Every tool call and agent message gets checked against policy first. 

The check takes 0.012ms and denies by default if anything errors.
 
Here is what it adds:
 
> Quantum-safe identities per agent
> Behavioral trust scoring out of 1000
> Four privilege rings with sandboxing
> Tamper-evident logs for audits
> Kill switch for instant shutdown
 
The release ships with 992 conformance tests. 

Native hooks plug into LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, and Semantic Kernel.
 
Compliance mapping covers the EU AI Act, SOC 2, and HIPAA.
 
Everything is open-source and works with most agent frameworks.
 
Will prompt-based safety survive once this becomes a runtime layer?

items.title: Microservices break applications into smaller parts so systems can change without slowing everything...
items.content:
Microservices break applications into smaller parts so systems can change without slowing everything down. 

As complexity grows, this approach helps teams update features faster, fix issues locally, and keep services running without large disruptions. 

Microblog by @antgrasso

What changes in practice:

- Teams work in parallel on different services, reducing delivery time

- Issues stay contained within a service instead of spreading system-wide

- Scaling targets only what is needed, improving resource use

- APIs make communication between services clearer and more structured

- New technologies can be introduced without rewriting the whole system

Strong coordination is still required to keep all services aligned and working together.

🖨️🖼️ Big progress: it can now print images too

I had to install a different printer driver, Claude Code recommended Epson FX-86e which supported images

Then I printed an image, it helped me dump a .bin from the printer raw data output, then it decoded the .bin and figured out how to draw images on a canvas (in the printer) from that data

So crazy I'd have no clue how to do this without AI ever!

items.content:
🖨️ It took over a year and lots of help from Claude Code but I have now been able to create a real dot matrix printer on the web that prints from Windows 3.11 in your browser:

Here's the test page: https://t.co/vYLW6mQpUC

I couldn't get the LPT1 port to work but COM2 did work (the dial up model already uses COM1), the JS receives the COM2 serial data (just raw print data) and then prints it

Next is to make the dot matrix printer better looking (kinda 3d like on pieter dot com) and have the paper also flowing in 3d out of the printer and integrate it on the https://t.co/M1hEUBAynC page

My idea was that it might work because we also capture the COM1 data (from the dial up modem) and send it to a Websocket as a dial up connection, so we should be able to capture the COM2 traffic too but I didn't know how, but now I do

 IT WORKS!! 😊

I couldn't get the LPT1 port to work but COM2 did work (the dial up model already uses COM1), the JS receives the COM2 serial data (just raw print data) and then prints it

Next is to make the dot matrix printer better looking (kinda 3d like on pieter dot com) and have the paper also flowing in 3d out of the printer and integrate it on the https://t.co/M1hEUBAynC page

My idea was that it might work because we also capture the COM1 data (from the dial up modem) and send it to a Websocket as a dial up connection, so we should be able to capture the COM2 traffic too but I didn't know how, but now I do

IT WORKS!! 😊

I used to be more excited by what Apple was about to launch. I get more excited now by enterpreneurs who are trying to bring us better tools. Particularly in developer tools, which is incredibly crowded, with 1,800 companies alone: https://t.co/pnC9ugEDCi

While we'll all use the new Siri to find a place to eat nearby, or figure out where we parked our car, I seriously doubt anyone will use it to build new AI infrastructure at their businesses, or switch from coding with OpenAI or Anthropic to using it. At best it might set that up for future work.

Google's model underneath thew new Siri will bring new multi-modality features that will be needed. But I don't expect it to take the energy away from people like Prasanna Sankar, @myprasanna, who I interview here about his new "smartest software engineer" system: https://t.co/rHJdvglN9p

This video is a deep dive into the next generation of AI-powered software development, centered on a conversation with Prasanna, founder of Vorflux and previously a founder of Rippling. The discussion explains why AI coding tools are moving beyond simple “vibe coding” and autocomplete toward more powerful cloud-based agent systems that can plan, build, test, review, and ship software with far less human micromanagement.

I learned long ago to watch the developers for the real innovation.

That said, sure hope Apple pulls something out that excites us, I just don't expect it. Do you? 

You will learn more about the future of software development by watching this, I expect, than WWDC tomorrow. 

But I'll be watching hoping I'm wrong.

items.content:
🖨️ Little progress: I generated a dot matrix printer with GPT Image 2 and then some Photopea'ing to make the paper go through

And I got COM1 to work for dial up internet to https://t.co/Dbxr5wovpF (my ISP) while COM2 still works for the printer (that was a challenge)

Which means I can now print anything from the internet, like Hacker News!

Some todo fixes:
- page should scroll out not already be there
- we need some perspective so the paper slowly goes into distance
- integrate it into pieter .com page fully
- add a local print command so you can actually IRL print your printed dot matrix print

You can try it the current WIP here: https://t.co/vYLW6mQpUC

items.title: @fieldsfamwines @satnam6502 True! And for those who don't know me, I had the first rides in these ca...
items.content:
@fieldsfamwines @satnam6502 True! And for those who don't know me, I had the first rides in these cars because I studied automotive innovation up close for decades due to two of my friends in high school getting killed in wrecks:

Fiat 500.
BMW i3.
Tesla Roaders (had first ride in first production car with Elon).
Mercedes AI car.

And early rides in Toyota Prius, which I owned for a decade (with advanced tech package, of course) and Mercedes with NVIDIA stack at GTC this year.

And have driven many cars, even old ones last year on a car rally with billionaires. And have been to many motor sports events, including where the first robot beat a professional human driver at @A2RLeague in Abu Dhabi. 

Was the first to write about Robotaxi in a book, too. Among other firsts. Had the first video of a Waymo on YouTube too. And NASCAR took me in its pace car and Audi taught me to race on both ice and concrete.

@AeroGennic @satnam6502 Well, there's a company called EVJECT which lets you drive off without getting out of your car to escape some bad person. But I've been all over America and I've never had an experience like that.

Usually it's another Tesla owner knocking on my window to talk to me about my car.

And yeah, if you haven't been in a new Tesla lately, they're dramatically better than they were back in 2017. 

The quality is just better in all ways:
1. Fit and finish is better
2. They're quieter inside
3. The suspensions are better
4. The sound system is better
5. The interior is better

But I'm still not giving up my 2018. I have one of the few Model 3s made with a real leather steering wheel.
